citation: Polletta J, Leung K, Diaz D, Branum N, Mokha M. Influence of interlimb lean muscle mass asymmetry on countermovement jump neuromuscular performance qualities among American football players. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research. 2024.
abstract: This cross-sectional correlational study investigated whether interlimb lean muscle mass (LMM) asymmetry measured via bioelectrical impedance analysis (InBody 270) was associated with countermovement jump (CMJ) neuromuscular performance asymmetries and overall jump performance in NFL draft preparation athletes. Seventy-four male American football players completed bilateral CMJ testing on VALD ForceDecks (1000 Hz) and body composition assessment. LMM asymmetry averaged 0.67±0.67%. Despite statistically significant right-left LMM differences (p<0.001), no significant correlations were found between LMM asymmetry and any CMJ asymmetry variable (eccentric peak force 4.77±3.75%, eccentric impulse 5.23±4.36%, concentric peak force 2.99±2.54%, concentric impulse 3.06±2.97%), jump height (41.8±7.3 cm), or reactive strength index modified (RSImod: 0.53±0.14 m/s). Eccentric phase asymmetries were consistently larger than concentric phase asymmetries.
tldr: Interlimb lean muscle mass asymmetry does not significantly predict CMJ force or impulse asymmetries, jump height, or RSImod in NFL draft prep football players, suggesting that structural (mass) imbalances alone do not drive functional neuromuscular asymmetries during bilateral jumping.
methods: Cross-sectional correlational design; N=74 male American football players (NFL draft preparation); body composition via InBody 270 BIA; bilateral CMJ on VALD ForceDecks at 1000 Hz; CMJ variables: eccentric peak force, eccentric impulse, concentric peak force, concentric impulse asymmetry indices, jump height, RSImod; Pearson/Spearman correlations between LMM asymmetry and CMJ variables.
findings: Right-left LMM was significantly different (p<0.001); mean LMM asymmetry = 0.67±0.67%. No significant correlations between LMM asymmetry and eccentric peak force asymmetry (4.77±3.75%), eccentric impulse asymmetry (5.23±4.36%), concentric peak force asymmetry (2.99±2.54%), concentric impulse asymmetry (3.06±2.97%), jump height (41.8±7.3 cm), or RSImod (0.53±0.14 m/s). Eccentric phase asymmetries were larger than concentric phase asymmetries.
limitations: Cross-sectional design prevents causal inference; sample limited to NFL draft prep athletes, reducing generalizability to other populations; BIA for LMM measurement is less precise than DXA; single CMJ protocol may not capture all functional asymmetry expressions; no injury history or training load data included.
practicalImplications: Practitioners should not use lean muscle mass asymmetry as a proxy for functional neuromuscular asymmetry in football players. CMJ force plate testing provides distinct and independent information about bilateral performance imbalances. Structural and functional assessments should be used in parallel rather than as substitutes for each other in athlete monitoring programs.
athleteDev: For developing football players, interlimb muscle mass symmetry programs should not be expected to directly normalize CMJ force or impulse asymmetries. Neuromuscular training targeting eccentric force production may be more effective for reducing functional asymmetries, given that eccentric phase asymmetries were consistently larger than concentric in this population.
rtp: CMJ force plate asymmetry data (eccentric and concentric peak force, impulse) provides independent RTP information beyond body composition symmetry measures. Lean muscle mass symmetry alone is insufficient as a RTP criterion for bilateral neuromuscular function; functional CMJ-based asymmetry indices should be assessed separately post-injury.

citation: Pecci J, Sañudo B, Ramirez-Campillo R, Saez de Villarreal E. Influence of resistance training variables and the Nordic hamstring exercise on biceps femoris architectural adaptations in soccer players: a systematic review with meta-analysis. Sports Health. 2025.
abstract: This systematic review with meta-analysis examined the effects of the Nordic hamstring exercise (NHE) and related resistance training variables on biceps femoris long head (BFlh) architectural adaptations — specifically fascicle length, muscle thickness, and pennation angle — in soccer players. Six studies encompassing 12 training groups and 168 participants met inclusion criteria. Meta-analysis revealed that NHE significantly increased BFlh fascicle length (P=0.01). Training frequency of 2x/week versus 1x/week showed no statistically significant difference in fascicle length adaptation but trended toward greater effect size with higher frequency. Studies using >290 total repetitions over 6–12 weeks, averaging ≥57 reps/week, produced significant fascicle length increases. Muscle thickness showed a nonsignificant increase and pennation angle a nonsignificant decrease. All included studies used bodyweight NHE; comparisons involving additional load, hip-dominant variations, or degree of effort were not possible due to insufficient data.
tldr: NHE training significantly increases BFlh fascicle length in soccer players, particularly when volume exceeds 290 total reps or 57 reps/week over 6–12 weeks. Frequency and volume appear more important than dose-matching, but insufficient data existed to compare loaded variations or hip-dominant exercises.
methods: Systematic review with meta-analysis; 6 studies, 12 training groups, N=168 soccer players; primary outcomes: BFlh fascicle length, muscle thickness, pennation angle measured via ultrasound; moderator analysis of training variables (frequency, volume, duration, intensity); PRISMA guidelines followed; all included studies used bodyweight NHE only.
findings: NHE significantly increased BFlh fascicle length (P=0.01). Volume threshold of >290 total reps over 6–12 weeks (≥57 reps/week) was associated with significant fascicle length gains. 2x/week frequency showed trend toward greater ES vs 1x/week but was not statistically significant. Muscle thickness increase and pennation angle decrease were nonsignificant. Loaded NHE and hip-dominant exercise comparisons not possible due to limited data.
limitations: Only 6 studies met inclusion criteria, limiting statistical power for moderator analyses; all studies used bodyweight NHE, preventing generalization to loaded or varied NHE protocols; heterogeneity in outcome measurement techniques across studies; no female soccer player data; intensity and degree of effort could not be compared; lack of injury outcome data.
practicalImplications: To drive BFlh fascicle length adaptation in soccer players, NHE programs should target a minimum of 290 total reps over a 6–12 week block, with at least 57 reps per week. Twice-weekly frequency is recommended as a practical target. Practitioners should monitor cumulative volume rather than session-by-session intensity as the primary programming driver for architectural adaptation.
athleteDev: Developing soccer and football players should incorporate NHE into preseason and in-season programs with sufficient volume (≥57 reps/week) to drive fascicle length adaptation. Fascicle length is a key architectural determinant of hamstring strain injury risk; volume-based NHE programming should be a foundational component of long-term athlete development plans.
rtp: Following BFlh strain in soccer players, NHE-based rehabilitation programs should include progressive volume targets approaching 57+ reps/week to restore fascicle length as part of criteria-based RTP. Ultrasound monitoring of BFlh architecture provides an objective metric for tracking rehabilitation-induced structural recovery alongside functional strength testing.

citation: Wannop JW, Kowalchuk S, Smith-Bregeda E, Aldahir P, Spratley EM, O'Cain CM, Park G, Stefanyshyn D. Influence of sport surface properties on utilized traction and lower extremity biomechanics of American football players. Applied Sciences. 2026;16:2423.
abstract: This study examined how three FieldTurf surface conditions — Control, Shockpad, and Low Faceweight — affected utilized traction, foot orientation, and lower extremity biomechanics during football-specific movement tasks. Sixteen university-level American football players (9 speed, 7 power positions) performed an agility drill (5-10-5), vcut (90° cut), and three-cone drill on each surface using a repeated-measures ANOVA design. Low Faceweight turf produced lower utilized traction (particularly at takeoff) and greater foot eversion compared to Control and Shockpad surfaces. Higher-traction surfaces (Control and Shockpad) produced flatter foot contact, greater ankle inversion, more foot torsion, and increased frontal and transverse plane ankle and knee moments. Speed position players demonstrated higher utilized traction, greater range of motion, and higher angular velocities compared to power position players across all surfaces. A minimum traction coefficient exceeding 1.6 at push-off was identified as necessary for maximal cutting performance. This study was NFL-funded.
tldr: Lower faceweight artificial turf reduces utilized traction and alters foot mechanics during football cutting drills, while higher-traction surfaces increase ankle and knee loading in frontal and transverse planes. Speed-position athletes place greater traction demands on surfaces than power-position athletes, with a minimum traction coefficient of >1.6 required for maximal cutting performance.
methods: Repeated-measures ANOVA; N=16 university football players (9 speed, 7 power); 3 FieldTurf surfaces: Control, Shockpad, Low Faceweight; 3 drills: agility (5-10-5), vcut (90° cut), three-cone; outcome variables: utilized traction coefficient, foot orientation (eversion/inversion), ankle/knee joint moments and ROM (frontal, transverse, sagittal planes), angular velocities; NFL-funded study.
findings: Low Faceweight surface: lower utilized traction at takeoff, greater foot eversion. Control and Shockpad (high-traction) surfaces: flatter foot contact, greater ankle inversion, more foot torsion, increased frontal/transverse plane ankle and knee moments. Speed players: higher utilized traction, greater ROM and angular velocities vs power players. Minimum traction coefficient >1.6 at push-off required for maximal cutting performance.
limitations: Small sample size (n=16); university-level players may not represent elite NFL populations; limited to three surface configurations from one manufacturer (FieldTurf); testing in controlled conditions may not reflect in-game variability; cleat type standardized across conditions but may interact with surface properties differently in practice.
practicalImplications: Facility and equipment staff should be aware that low faceweight turf reduces traction availability during cutting, potentially limiting maximal performance. Higher-traction surfaces increase frontal and transverse plane joint loading at the ankle and knee, which has injury risk implications. Traction coefficient >1.6 at push-off should be a minimum surface performance standard. Speed-position players require surface traction optimization more critically than power-position players.
athleteDev: Developing football players should train and compete on surfaces that provide adequate traction (>1.6 coefficient at push-off) to enable full expression of cutting mechanics. Speed-position athletes (WRs, CBs, RBs) are more sensitive to surface traction variation than power-position athletes (OL, DL). Surface selection for practice facilities should account for position-specific traction demands.
rtp: Following lower extremity injury in football players, surface traction properties should be considered in the graduated RTP progression. Initiating cutting and agility work on lower-traction surfaces may reduce frontal/transverse plane joint loading during early rehabilitation phases, with progression to higher-traction game-surface conditions as criteria-based milestones are met.

citation: Clements E, Ehrmann F, Clark A, Jones M, McCall A, Duffield R. Influence of travel demands and match load on recovery following postmatch travel in national-team footballers. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance. 2025.
abstract: This observational cohort study examined the combined influence of match load (GPS-derived) and postmatch travel characteristics on perceptual recovery in national-team male footballers over five international seasons (October 2018–November 2023). Seventy-nine players completed 258 travel trips. GPS match load variables and perceptual monitoring (fatigue, muscle soreness, sleep quality, stress, recovery on 7-point Likert scales and Perceived Recovery Scale) were assessed through MD+3. Linear mixed models identified key predictors. Higher match decelerations were associated with worse fatigue and soreness at MD+3. Higher very-high-speed distance (VHSD) was associated with worse sleep quality and recovery. Return travel from national team to club was associated with significantly worse fatigue (+0.491 AU), soreness (+0.297 AU), and recovery (−0.629 AU) versus transition travel. Eastward travel was associated with worse sleep quality (+0.429 AU). Later match departure times worsened sleep quality at MD+3 (0.024 AU/hour). Longer travel duration was associated with worse recovery at MD+3 (−0.065 AU/hour).
tldr: Match deceleration load and VHSD impair perceptual recovery to MD+3 in national-team footballers, while return travel (national team to club) compounds recovery disruption beyond the match itself. Eastward travel and late post-match departures further worsen sleep quality, highlighting combined sport-travel load as a key recovery management target.
methods: Observational cohort study; N=79 male national-team footballers; 258 travel trips across 5 seasons (Oct 2018–Nov 2023); GPS match load monitoring; perceptual monitoring via 7-point Likert scales (fatigue, soreness, sleep quality, stress, recovery) + Perceived Recovery Scale (PRS); linear mixed models; assessment points: MD+1, MD+2, MD+3.
findings: Higher match decelerations → worse fatigue and soreness at MD+3. Higher VHSD → worse sleep quality and recovery at MD+3. Return travel (national team to club): +0.491 AU fatigue, +0.297 AU soreness, −0.629 AU recovery vs transition travel. Eastward travel: +0.429 AU worse sleep quality. Later departure post-match: 0.024 AU/hour worsening in sleep quality at MD+3. Longer travel duration: −0.065 AU/hour recovery at MD+3.
limitations: Observational design limits causal inference; perceptual monitoring is subjective and may be influenced by contextual factors beyond travel; GPS metrics may not fully capture all match load dimensions (e.g., contact, collision load in American football); findings specific to national-team travel schedules which differ from club or NFL travel patterns; no objective sleep or biological recovery markers.
practicalImplications: National team and club medical staff should monitor combined match load (particularly decelerations and VHSD) and travel characteristics to anticipate recovery disruptions. Return travel windows post-national team duty require specific recovery management protocols. Eastward travel and late-night post-match departures should be minimized or proactively managed with sleep hygiene and circadian adjustment strategies.
athleteDev: Developing international footballers should be educated on the compounding effects of match load and travel on recovery, particularly the distinct challenges of return travel from national team camps. Recovery literacy — including sleep strategies for eastward travel and late departures — should be incorporated into athlete development curricula at the academy and professional level.
rtp: For footballers returning from national team duty post-injury, the combined burden of match load and international travel should be factored into RTP timelines. Return travel significantly impairs perceptual recovery beyond match load alone; clearance decisions for post-international window returns should account for accumulated travel fatigue, not just match participation metrics.

citation: Isenmann E, Veit S, Flenker U, Lesch A, Lachenmeier DW, Diel P. Short-term chronic oral cannabidiol (CBD) supplementation on muscle recovery and performance after intensive training. Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition. 2024;21(1):2337252.
abstract: This triple-crossover randomized controlled trial examined the effects of short-term chronic oral CBD supplementation (60 mg/day over 6 days) on muscle recovery biomarkers and performance following an intensive shock microcycle in well-trained athletes. Seventeen participants (15 male, 2 female) completed three conditions: placebo, 60 mg CBD oil, and 60 mg CBD solubilisate. The microcycle included 3 strength sessions and 3 HIIT sessions over 6 days. Performance measures included CMJ, back squat 1RM, bench press 1RM, and 1-mile run. Biomarkers assessed were CK, myoglobin, IL-6, IL-10, OxLDL, ImAnOx, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and systemic immune-inflammation index (SII). Athletes were classified as advanced (Ad, n=8) or highly advanced (Hi, n=9) based on back squat relative strength. CK increased significantly in both groups regardless of treatment. CBD oil significantly reduced the myoglobin increase in the advanced group (p=0.049). No treatment effects were observed on performance parameters. No significant effects on inflammatory markers were found. PLR decreased in the advanced group under placebo; CBD oil showed a trend toward inhibiting this decrease. Performance level was identified as a decisive moderating factor in CBD response.
tldr: 60 mg/day oral CBD supplementation over a 6-day shock microcycle reduced myoglobin elevation in advanced (but not highly advanced) athletes without affecting CK, inflammatory markers, or any performance metric (CMJ, squat, bench, run), suggesting CBD has limited and performance-level-dependent effects on muscle damage recovery in well-trained athletes.
methods: Triple-crossover RCT; N=17 well-trained athletes (15M, 2F); conditions: placebo vs 60 mg CBD oil vs 60 mg CBD solubilisate; 6-day shock microcycle (3 strength + 3 HIIT sessions); performance: CMJ, back squat 1RM, bench press 1RM, 1-mile run; biomarkers: CK, myoglobin, IL-6, IL-10, OxLDL, ImAnOx, NLR, PLR, SII; subjects stratified by performance level: advanced (Ad, n=8) vs highly advanced (Hi, n=9).
findings: CK increased significantly in all conditions regardless of treatment. CBD oil significantly reduced myoglobin increase in the advanced group (p=0.049); no effect in highly advanced group. No significant treatment effects on CMJ, squat 1RM, bench 1RM, or 1-mile run. No significant effects on IL-6, IL-10, OxLDL, ImAnOx, NLR, or SII. PLR decreased in Ad group under placebo; CBD oil trended toward inhibiting this decrease. Performance level was a key moderating variable.
limitations: Small sample size (n=17) limits statistical power; female underrepresentation (n=2) limits sex-based generalizability; 6-day microcycle design may not reflect typical training loads; CBD dose (60 mg/day) may be subtherapeutic for highly advanced athletes; crossover design assumes adequate washout but CBD pharmacokinetics are variable; no blinding verification reported.
practicalImplications: CBD supplementation at 60 mg/day appears to have limited practical benefit for muscle recovery or performance in well-trained football athletes. The modest myoglobin-reduction effect in intermediate-level athletes does not translate to meaningful performance outcomes. Given the lack of effect on CK, inflammation, and performance, CBD cannot be recommended as a primary recovery supplement for high-performance football players based on current evidence.
athleteDev: Developing football athletes should not prioritize CBD supplementation as a recovery strategy over established methods (sleep, nutrition, structured recovery modalities). The performance-level dependency of any observed CBD effect suggests that as athletes advance in training status, CBD's marginal benefits diminish further. Evidence-based recovery education should focus on sleep quality, carbohydrate and protein periodization, and progressive load management.
rtp: CBD supplementation does not appear to accelerate return to performance after intensive training loads in well-trained athletes based on this RCT. No significant effects were found on functional performance markers (CMJ, strength) or key inflammatory biomarkers relevant to RTP decision-making. CBD use post-injury should not be positioned as an evidence-based RTP accelerant in football settings based on current literature.
